mistakes: missing return on recursive call
wrong recursive relationship
qualitative.key_insight: base case is n===0, recursive case is n + sumToN(n-1)
qualitative.coach_notes: 3 attempts to mastery — pattern recognition wasn't there yet

qualitative.key_insight: pattern recognition kicking in from Sum 1 to N
qualitative.coach_notes: first attempt success — template internalized

qualitative.key_insight: reduce by n/2 instead of n-1 — same pattern, different progress mechanism
qualitative.coach_notes: first attempt success

mistakes: base case indexing error — n===1,n===2 instead of n===0,n===1
qualitative.key_insight: call tree visualization made overlapping subproblems obvious — fib(2) calculated twice, fib(1) three times
qualitative.coach_notes: visualization was highest-leverage moment — abstract concept clicked when made visual

mistakes: if(cache[n]) instead of if(n in cache) — falsy zero bug
qualitative.key_insight: store and reuse values instead of recomputing — self-articulated after seeing call tree
qualitative.coach_notes: self-caught the cache bug — the specific guided question unlocked it. 1.6M× speedup demonstrated (fib(50): 60s → 0.037ms)

mistakes: wrong function name in recursive call — caught himself when tests failed
empty string edge case caused infinite recursion — caught via tracing
qualitative.key_insight: both versions are O(n²) due to string concatenation — not because of slice. Raised this himself and concluded recursive string reversal is pointless in production
qualitative.coach_notes: strong self-advocacy — flagged that complexity analysis had been omitted and asked for it always

mistakes: triangle[i]=[1] on last element wipes the whole row — caught after one targeted trace question
said n(n-1)/2 instead of n(n+1)/2 for total cells formula
qualitative.key_insight: one is creating an element in the parent array, another is updating an element of the parent array
qualitative.coach_notes: tracing is the primary unblocking tool — one targeted question unlocked the overwrite bug himself

mistakes: inner loop started at row.length-1 instead of row.length-2 — caught via tracing i=1
qualitative.key_insight: right-to-left avoids overwriting values still needed. The newly pushed 1 should never be touched — start at row.length-2
qualitative.coach_notes: session was split (2am on a train) — still shipped it. Right-to-left in-place pattern recurs in knapsack, edit distance

mistakes: reverse loop condition left===right instead of left<right
break outside the if block — would always break after first outer loop iteration
mistook sequential loops for nested — called O(n²) instead of O(n)
qualitative.key_insight: never independently articulated one — full guided walkthrough. Understood each step when explained but could not derive algorithm cold
qualitative.coach_notes: explicit plan to redo cold after more two-pointer reps. Flag: sequential vs nested loop complexity

mistakes: if instead of while for skipping — only skips one non-alphanumeric
bounds check in wrong order — regex evaluated before the guard
qualitative.key_insight: left<right guard must come first — short-circuit evaluation means regex never runs when pointers have crossed
qualitative.coach_notes: 5th percentile runtime due to regex. Revisit with charCodeAt optimization. Recursive version deferred by Safwaan

mistakes: typo: num[i] instead of nums[i] — caught himself during trace
qualitative.key_insight: time-space trade-off: brute force O(n²)/O(1), hash map O(n)/O(n). Buying speed with memory
qualitative.coach_notes: came in planning to use two pointers — correctly identified himself that array is unsorted and two pointers don't apply

mistakes: declared left and right as const instead of let
multiple if checks instead of else if — caught the runtime difference himself (61st → 100th percentile)
qualitative.key_insight: sorted array: moving left always decreases sum, moving right always increases it. Without that guarantee you're just guessing — self-articulated
qualitative.coach_notes: needed nudge to reach for two pointers — wouldn't have identified pattern cold. But once given the sorted property insight, derived the rest independently

pattern.primary: Two pointers — both ends, fill backwards

mistakes: forgot to sort in naive solution (low sleep)
tried to place two elements per iteration — caught himself via tracing
left<right instead of left<=right — needed example, fixed immediately
right declared without let/const (implicit global) — not caught
qualitative.key_insight: largest squares always at the ends — never the middle. Compare both ends, place largest at back, one element per iteration
qualitative.coach_notes: low sleep day. Implicit global variable not caught — watch for this. Complexity reasoning improving — used pointer movement to reason O(n), not loop syntax

pattern.primary: Two pointers — converge, greedy move shorter

mistakes: width formula (j+1)-(i+1) instead of j-i — caught after trace of i=0,j=1
couldn't fully justify pointer movement without guided Socratic chain
qualitative.key_insight: moving the taller pointer is provably wasteful — height capped at shorter, width only shrinks. Only moving shorter gives any chance of improvement. Self-articulated after guided chain
qualitative.coach_notes: pointer movement justification still guided — probe this cold next time. left<right reasoning was solid and independent

mistakes: result.push[...] instead of result.push(...) — caught when asked to look at the push call
forgot to sort nums before two-pointer loop
break after first match — never finds additional triplets for same i
triplet declared outside while loop, never reset
skip conditions checked wrong neighbor — self-diagnosed correctly
single if instead of while for consecutive duplicates — flagged himself before it was raised
qualitative.key_insight: fix one element, two-pointer the rest for a pair summing to -nums[i]. Sort first — duplicates become adjacent so dedup is free. O(n²) time, O(1) extra space
qualitative.coach_notes: closes subproblems at first success — surfaced 3x. Independently decomposed to k-sum and called O(n³)/O(n²) unprompted. Toolkit gap: Set reference equality, Array.from not at fingertips

mistakes: typo num2Pointer
while condition <= instead of >=
read from nums1 instead of nums2 for nums2PointerValue
assigned pointer index instead of value
qualitative.key_insight: fill from the right — currentIndex always stays ahead of nums1Pointer so you never overwrite a value you still need
qualitative.coach_notes: all bugs self-caught via tracing. Slow start due to placeholder zeros confusion. Pattern transfer from LC 977 happened with one recall probe.

mistakes: missing p1++ for non-target case — infinite loop
double p1++ after fix — caught by tracing
termination condition swappableIndex===0 instead of <=p1 — required 40min trace to expose
return value n-valuesSwapped instead of p1 — self-caught from tracing [3,3,2]
qualitative.key_insight: loop must terminate when pointers cross (swappableIndex<=p1), not when one hits zero. Return p1 — it is the count of valid elements. Read/write: write pointer only moves on a write; safe to overwrite because write never gets ahead of read
qualitative.coach_notes: swap approach designed cold — harder of the two solutions. Tracing heuristic taught: use smallest input that exposes the bug. Read/write pattern new, derived in one attempt after setup.

mistakes: reached for swap/move-to-end approach initially — approach mismatch
used const for variable reassigned in inner while — flagged by question, not self-caught
unnecessary inner while loop — removed after one question
qualitative.key_insight: p2 reads every step; p1 only writes when nums[p2] !== nums[p1]. Sorted array means duplicates are adjacent, so one pass is enough.
qualitative.coach_notes: pattern-matched to LC 27 swap variant immediately — pivoted correctly once dead end was recognised. const reassignment habit recurring quietly.

problem.name: Remove Duplicates from Sorted Array II

mistakes: group thinking — tracked occurrence counts and wrote on transition instead of per-element
spent significant time patching broken approach instead of scrapping it
qualitative.key_insight: think per-element not per-group: skip when both nums[p1-1] and nums[p1-2] equal the current value
qualitative.coach_notes: group thinking named as new mistake pattern. Bootstrap condition (p1<2) was self-observed. Return value (p1 not p1+1) self-reasoned.

mistakes: for...in keys are strings — needed Number(key), caught after one prompt
forgot for...in syntax cold (had to look up)
qualitative.key_insight: Boyer-Moore: keep one candidate and count; mismatch cancels a vote; majority survives because it appears more than half the time
qualitative.coach_notes: short clean session; pushed back on written trace after tests passed — accepted. Boyer-Moore couldn't be derived cold; correctly self-diagnosed.

mistakes: missed n=0 on first two iterative attempts
missed negative n on first attempt
called myPow(x, n/2) twice instead of storing — double work
scoped powerOfHalfed inside if/else — inaccessible outside
n/2 without Math.floor for odd n — float causes infinite recursion
qualitative.key_insight: x^n = x^(n/2) * x^(n/2) — halving the exponent each step gives O(log n); store the half once, square it; odd n adds one extra x
qualitative.coach_notes: pattern was brand new — he exhausted his toolbox honestly and asked for help. Good metacognition. Math intuition solid; knowledge gap, not reasoning gap.

mistakes: if(seen[currentElement]) fails for 0 — falsy check on plain object, caught when prompted
qualitative.key_insight: Set for membership tracking — no falsy edge case, no string conversion overhead. HashMap when you also need to store a value.
qualitative.coach_notes: Reached for HashMap directly without brute force — pattern recognition shift. Independently identified Set as cleaner choice. Self-articulated Set vs HashMap distinction.

mistakes: wrong index in right scan (height[rightMaxHeight] instead of height[rightStartIndex])
infinite loop in left scan — leftStartIndex not decrementing in else branch
two-pointer condition (leftMax <= rightMax) not derived — got from external chat
qualitative.key_insight: min(maxLeft, maxRight) - height[i]; precompute both maxes in two passes to eliminate inner scans; two-pointer processes the bottleneck side without needing both maxes upfront
qualitative.coach_notes: prefix max derivation was entirely independent and clean — highlight of the session. Two-pointer condition gap acknowledged honestly. Hard redo scheduled 2026-06-13.

problem.name: Best Time to Buy and Sell Stock

mistakes: last-day edge case on brute force — maxSellingPrice stays -1 when inner loop never runs
tried to fix decreasing prices by corrupting maxSellingPrice to 0 inside inner loop
if/else instead of Math.max() — self-identified habit
qualitative.key_insight: scan left to right, track running minimum, profit at each step = current - minPrice, keep the best
qualitative.coach_notes: needed full trace walkthrough before code structure clicked; abstract understanding was there but could not start the loop. Self-identified Math.max() avoidance habit.

mistakes: copy-paste variable name in t branch (currSChar instead of currTChar)
else-branch initial value for t was 1 instead of -1
comparison loop iterated s only, missing t-only characters with negative counts
qualitative.key_insight: one-map comparison must iterate Object.keys() not just s — t-only characters accumulate negative values that never get checked otherwise
qualitative.coach_notes: clean session; increment/decrement insight clicked fast when suggested; comparison loop bug needed a counter-example to land

pattern.primary: Hash Map — sorted string as key

mistakes: swapped if/else branches — creating array in the exists branch, pushing in the new branch
qualitative.key_insight: sort each string's characters to get a canonical key — all anagrams produce the same sorted form; use as HashMap key, push originals into the array at that key
qualitative.coach_notes: sorted-key insight needed to be given; first instinct was O(n²) pairwise using isAnagram as helper. Once insight landed, execution was clean.

mistakes: reset subString to empty instead of slicing from dupIndex+1
maxLength not updated in else branch
currentWindowSize computed before left updated
left = map[char]+1 without Math.max — pointer jumping backwards
const on reassigned variables (recurring)
pulled Set solution from online mid-session
qualitative.key_insight: sliding window: expand right always, shrink left when duplicate found; HashMap stores char→last index for O(1) jump; Math.max(left, map[char]+1) ensures left never goes backwards
qualitative.coach_notes: window concept needed visual trace before code clicked; Math.max guard was given not derived — probe cold at LC 424

qualitative.key_insight: count what ransomNote needs, spend magazine characters against it — positive count means magazine came up short
qualitative.coach_notes: fastest clean solve of the sprint. Explored 5 variants. Added splice, charCodeAt, ?? vs || to toolkit.

mistakes: one-map namespace collision — s-side key shadowed t-side has-check
map.set before map.has — wrong order in else branch
tMapping comparison used currentTChar instead of currentSChar
stale variable name map.set after renaming to sMapping/tMapping
qualitative.key_insight: s-side keys and t-side keys live in separate namespaces — one map collapses them; two maps keeps them clean; check for conflict then set unconditionally
qualitative.coach_notes: namespace collision concept took a full trace of paper/title to make concrete; clean two-line pattern shown at end and immediately understood

mistakes: .set() argument order — used wrong variable as key, omitted value in both maps
missing n !== s.length length check — self-caught before submitting
qualitative.key_insight: two-map bijection transfers directly from LC 205; only difference is s.split(" ") to get words array
qualitative.coach_notes: bijection pattern landing; why-one-map-fails answered cold and perfectly before attempting

mistakes: window.size > k+1 too loose — allows k+2 elements before shrinking
deleted currentElement instead of nums[l] when shrinking
initial confusion: tried to store all previous indices per value instead of just most recent
qualitative.key_insight: maintain a Set window of size k; shrink when r-l>k by deleting nums[l]; for HashMap store value→last index and always update it
qualitative.coach_notes: went back to notes/video mid-session when HashMap wasn't clicking — good metacognition. Did verbose-to-terse HashMap compression independently across 3 versions.

mistakes: = instead of += in accumulator — self-caught after one question
new Set(n) instead of new Set([n]) — Set takes an iterable
used .push() and .length on a Set — Set has .add() and .size
starting value -1 instead of n
forgot String() conversion after removing array
passed original n instead of currentValue to sumOfSquares helper
did not independently connect loop-forever to repeated-value — needed one nudge
qualitative.key_insight: cycle detection = track seen states in a Set; digit extraction via % 10 and Math.floor avoids string allocation
qualitative.coach_notes: one nudge needed for cycle detection insight; immediately reached for Set and avoided O(n) includes on his own once prompted

mistakes: const on mutable accumulators
for...in on a Set instead of for...of
elem.get() and seen.get() on a Set
forgot to advance curr (would have caused infinite loop)
forgot to reset runningSequenceCount
forgot return statement
qualitative.key_insight: only count from elements where n-1 is not in Set; inner while amortizes to O(n) total across all sequences
qualitative.coach_notes: for...in vs for...of explicitly flagged as a gap — needs deliberate drilling; Set API confusion (.get() on Set) recurred from 3Sum

qualitative.key_insight: a^a=0 and a^0=a, combined with XOR's commutativity/associativity, means XOR-ing the whole array cancels every duplicate pair and leaves only the single survivor
qualitative.coach_notes: first zero-mistake, zero-hint session of the sprint; directly attributable to having pre-built a bit manipulation cheat sheet before attempting the problem; bonus problem pulled forward from Phase 18 given interview proximity

mistakes: assumed bitwise operators require explicit decimal-to-binary conversion
missing reassignment on right shift (n >>> 1; instead of n = n >>> 1;)
qualitative.key_insight: integers are already stored in binary internally — bitwise operators act on that representation directly, no conversion step needed; n & 1 reads the last bit, n = n >>> 1 advances to the next
qualitative.coach_notes: second bit-manipulation problem same day as LC 136; needed direct correction on the binary-conversion misconception (knowledge gap, not reasoning gap); self-caught the missing-assignment bug immediately when prompted; explicitly self-identified log-based complexity reasoning as a weak spot and needed a full walkthrough from O(log n) to the O(1) fixed-width framing

mistakes: confused rotation with reversal
proposed OR-then-shift instead of shift-then-OR
proposed OR-then-right-shift (discards bits)
while(n!==0) terminated before all 32 positions processed
used n >> 1 instead of n >>> 1
qualitative.key_insight: reversing mirrors bit positions (i -> n-1-i), not rotates; building a positioned result requires shift-then-OR (never the reverse) and a fixed 32-iteration loop rather than a while(n!==0) loop
qualitative.coach_notes: first bit-manipulation bonus needing real debugging; trace tables (not verbal explanation) were the explicit unlock for the shift/OR ordering confusion — Safwaan confirmed this directly and asked for more of it; also independently read the actual problem constraints (n is even) and correctly reasoned why >>> 0 wasn't needed for this variant; strong instinct shown pushing back on a fix before accepting it

mistakes: conditional add instead of unconditional expand
recording after while loop (invalid window)
while condition > instead of >=
missing Infinity to 0 return
qualitative.key_insight: always add nums[end] unconditionally, then shrink while sum >= target — the add is never conditional
qualitative.coach_notes: needed explicit scaffold to get expand-always structure; zero self-caught bugs; first session back after ~1 week break

problem.name: Substring with Concatenation of All Words

pattern.primary: Sliding Window — Fixed Size, word frequency

mistakes: character frequency instead of word frequency (anagram instinct misapplied)
words[i] undefined reference in window size
words[1] instead of words[0].length
copied words into windowFreq instead of building from s
j += 2 instead of j++
wrong substring args (missing i offset, end vs length)
wordFreq === windowFreq reference comparison
s.subString wrong capitalization
off-by-one: < s.length - k missed last valid position
qualitative.key_insight: atomic unit is words not characters; all words same length so slice s into wordLen chunks at i + j*wordLen; compare word-frequency maps entry by entry
qualitative.coach_notes: anagram instinct is sticky — watch for correct vs incorrect application at LC 567 (which IS character-based); Map comparison was a complete gap, gave isMapsEqual directly; fixed vs variable window distinction confused him

pattern.primary: DFS — Accumulate Over Tree (iterative pairs + recursive post-order)

qualitative.key_insight: direct pattern transfer from max-root-to-leaf-path-sum: push [node, depth] pairs so branches carry independent state; recursive: Math.max(left, right) + 1, null → 0
qualitative.coach_notes: cleanest solve to date — zero hints, zero false starts, both approaches written simultaneously. Introduced balanced vs skewed tree terminology during complexity discussion.

mistakes: flattening to arrays loses structure
short-circuit return true before checking subtrees
missing null base case
qualitative.key_insight: matching values at the current node is not enough — must verify both subtrees too. Null+null = both trees ended at the same point = agree
qualitative.coach_notes: base case reasoning unlocked by concrete call-stack trace, not verbal explanation. Short-circuit bug is same shape as closing-subproblem-at-first-success pattern.

qualitative.key_insight: swap current node children first (pre-order), then recurse — post-order and BFS also work, same result
qualitative.coach_notes: self-identified recursive returns as a gap; independently questioned traversal order and whether other approaches work — good metacognitive habit forming

qualitative.key_insight: mirror = cross-compare children: left.left↔right.right (outside) and left.right↔right.left (inside). One line different from Same Tree.
qualitative.coach_notes: needed concept explained visually; independently asked for alternative approaches and algorithm names — good metacognitive habit forming

qualitative.key_insight: recursive: subtract as you go down — at the leaf, targetSum should equal root.val. Iterative: push [node, sum] pairs, check at leaves only.
qualitative.coach_notes: iterative was cold and correct — strong pattern transfer. BFS variant not implemented — open question to close next time.

mistakes: used ^ (XOR) instead of ** for exponentiation
qualitative.key_insight: if all-left height equals all-right height from same root, tree is perfect — return 2**h - 1 without traversing; recurse otherwise; shortcut fires at multiple levels
qualitative.coach_notes: three O(n) solutions cold in a row — DFS/BFS traversal genuinely internalized; optimal needed Socratic guidance to derive the complete-tree property insight

mistakes: leaf base case instead of null guard
return 1+left+right instead of 1+max(left,right)
couldnt identify second argument to Math.max for best update
qualitative.key_insight: two outputs at every node: best tracks left+right (full path through node), return sends 1+max(left,right) (single arm to parent)
qualitative.coach_notes: abstract-to-code bridge gap again; needed scaffold before loop could start; visualizer built mid-session to reinforce

mistakes: mixed return types (boolean + number)
height = 1+left+right instead of 1+max(left,right)
one-directional imbalance check (missed Math.abs)
qualitative.key_insight: sentinel value: return -1 instead of false to keep return type uniform; propagate -1 before any other logic
qualitative.coach_notes: sentinel concept needed explanation; once named, implementation was clean. Voluntarily tried closure variant afterward — good initiative

mistakes: Math.min null-child zero-return bug (self-caught)
qualitative.key_insight: Math.min breaks when one child is missing because null returns 0 — 0 means no child, not depth zero. If one side is 0, use the other side.
qualitative.coach_notes: self-diagnosed the bug before being asked; clean session with one nudge needed to code the fix

qualitative.key_insight: root.val goes first in the combine step: [root.val, ...left, ...right]
qualitative.coach_notes: self-solved outside coaching session; brought to session for wrap-up/logging only

qualitative.key_insight: root.val goes in the middle: [...left, root.val, ...right]
qualitative.coach_notes: self-solved outside coaching session; brought to session for wrap-up/logging only

qualitative.key_insight: root.val goes last: [...left, ...right, root.val]; same shape as Diameter/Balanced Tree combine step
qualitative.coach_notes: self-solved outside coaching session; brought to session for wrap-up/logging only

mistakes: believed 4 sequential recursive calls = BFS layer (corrected via call-stack trace)
counting explanation imprecise — had 'visited avoids re-traversal' but not 'exactly one flood origin per island'
qualitative.key_insight: Stack → DFS, queue → BFS; the data structure holding pending work defines the traversal, not how the code reads. Exactly one cell per island returns true — the flood origin.
qualitative.coach_notes: video-assisted solve, flagged honestly by Safwaan; declined own-words explanation at wrap-up — recognition vs ownership concern; short-fuse cold redo 2026-07-19 with mandatory verbal walkthrough before coding

pattern.primary: Graph DFS — Region Decision (walk full region, decide after)
pattern.secondary: Border-first flood fill (alternative, not yet owned)

mistakes: initial plan checked per-cell edge position instead of per-region connectivity
inverted bounds check (checked neighbor bounds instead of own bounds) — needed direct answer
r/c vs i/j typo carried over from LC 200 template
incomplete border condition (missed far edges) until traced on a corner cell
return false inside nested loop would have exited entire solve() function, not just the region decision
qualitative.key_insight: Surrounded is a region property, not a cell property; must walk the full region even after knowing the verdict, because visited protects the outer loop from redundant re-exploration; decide-then-act as two separate passes
qualitative.coach_notes: strong contrast to same-day LC 200 video-solve — nearly fully self-derived via Socratic tracing; pushed back fairly on being asked to re-explain an already-fully-stated plan (logged as pattern #43); explicitly flagged that the border-first flood fill alternative is not yet understood, needs follow-up at LC 417

mistakes: checked only out-degree 0, missing the in-degree n-1 condition entirely
outDegreeCount[b]++ typo instead of [a]++ (self-caught)
= instead of === in compound boolean condition
looped over map keys instead of 1..n, missing isolated nodes
missing ?? 0 default for undefined counts, could not recall the ?? operator
qualitative.key_insight: Judge requires both out-degree 0 AND in-degree n-1 — a two-sided condition; loop 1..n not map keys so isolated people are checked; default missing counts with ?? 0
qualitative.coach_notes: third problem of the day, session ran to ~3am; needed increasingly direct answers as it went on, likely fatigue not a fresh gap (especially the ?? recall, which he'd used correctly before in Ransom Note); flagging pacing to Safwaan next session

qualitative.feel: smooth, energy recovered from prior session
mistakes: called _Node constructor without new
passed original (un-cloned) neighbors array directly into clone
loop variable shadowed outer node parameter
registered clone in map after building neighbor list instead of before, despite correctly stating the rule verbally first
qualitative.key_insight: Register a node's clone the instant it is discovered, before recursing further — same principle as LC 200's mark-before-recurse. DFS vs BFS is purely which end of the pending-work list you pull from — proven via a one-line diff (queue.shift() vs stack.pop()) between two otherwise-identical solutions
qualitative.coach_notes: fourth problem of the day, energy fully recovered from the late LC 997 session; built and compared three full solutions (recursive DFS, iterative BFS, iterative DFS) at his request; wrote all three into index.js with comments and built a three-way comparison visualiser

pattern.primary: Weighted Graph DFS — search + accumulate with sentinel

qualitative.feel: started at zero, graph modeling clicked fast, DFS combining logic genuinely hard
mistakes: destructured weight but never used it in the multiplication
const reassignment
multiplied results of all neighbor branches together instead of returning on first success
no failure sentinel initially, dead-end branches returned a false success value
qualitative.key_insight: Each equation models as two directed edges (value + reciprocal). DFS must accumulate a running product AND use a -1 sentinel to distinguish a successful path from a dead end, returning immediately on success rather than continuing to combine branches
qualitative.coach_notes: fifth problem of the day; fully self-derived the entire graph model from I-have-no-idea with zero hints, but the core DFS combining logic needed to be given directly after repeated stuck attempts; complexity reasoning needed piece-by-piece scaffolding like LC 191 but assembled correctly once broken down; introduced Union-Find conceptually as a preview, not implemented

mistakes: const reassignment
missing water-check guard, both self-caught via one trace each
all-water edge case needed maxArea initialized to 0 not -Infinity
qualitative.key_insight: Flood fill generalizes to accumulating any region property, not just a boolean signal — return 1 + sum of 4 recursive calls instead of true/false
qualitative.coach_notes: deliberate ownership test for LC 200's flood-fill pattern (video-assisted origin, recognition-not-ownership concern) — passed cleanly with zero mention of LC 200 beforehand; meaningfully de-risks that earlier concern; also built mutate-in-place alternative, jumped from 22nd/48th to 77th/87th percentile

pattern.secondary: One-time-push mechanism (boolean flag / structural loop split)

qualitative.feel: opened as a total blank, unlocked through Socratic questioning, finished with unprompted code-quality curiosity
mistakes: const reassignment on newIntervalAdded
Math.min used instead of Math.max in merge formula
missing post-loop push when newInterval never triggers the after case
initial code nested the one-time-push logic as a sub-case of overlap instead of a parallel case
qualitative.key_insight: Classify each existing interval into before/overlap/after relative to newInterval in one pass; overlap grows newInterval via min/max; the merged interval must be pushed exactly once, either via a boolean flag or by restructuring into three sequential while loops where the push is structurally guaranteed to happen once
qualitative.coach_notes: revisit queue overdue since 2026-06-18, explicitly offered as a choice at session start, Safwaan chose to proceed with new material instead — now deferred across nine straight sessions; strong new signal at the end of the session: unprompted asked about code cleanliness separate from complexity, then correctly reasoned through a loop invariant when pushed on it

qualitative.feel: faster than LC 57, strong transfer of prior session's insights, energy dropped during the code-cleanliness refactor discussion
mistakes: assumed left-to-right scan works without checking sort order
first stated overlap condition was abstract and meaningless (current.start <= next.end)
missing final push for currentInterval after the loop (same shape as LC 57 bug)
initially used <= instead of strict < for the no-overlap check, self-corrected
qualitative.key_insight: Sort by start first since input isn't guaranteed ordered; once sorted, adjacent [a,b]/[c,d] always has a<=c, so only b>=c matters and merged start is always a; track currentInterval, push on a genuine gap plus one trailing push after the loop
qualitative.coach_notes: revisit queue declined a second straight session, this time stated directly rather than offered as a choice; strong cross-session transfer of loop-invariant reasoning and the code-cleanliness curiosity from LC 57, both now confirmed as generalizing habits rather than one-off spikes; self-connected the missing-final-push bug to the identical LC 57 bug unprompted; closed with a genuine metacognitive question about why the redundant-state refactor didn't occur to him first

pattern.primary: Intervals — Classify-and-Merge Single Pass (reduced, overlap check only)

qualitative.feel: fast and clean, most independent Intervals session yet
mistakes: const current reassigned inside the loop, 5th+ recurrence of this exact bug shape
qualitative.key_insight: Sort by start, then a single pass only needs to compare each interval to the one immediately before it — strict < for overlap since touching intervals are allowed. Both the length<=1 guard and the separate current/next variables turned out to be unnecessary once traced.
qualitative.coach_notes: revisit queue deferred a third straight session, but this time with a concrete 'next week' commitment rather than an open-ended defer — hold him to it next session. Sorted the input unprompted on the first draft, answering an open carry-forward question from LC 56. Code-cleanliness curiosity now confirmed across three straight sessions. Declined to answer the wrap-up reflection questions himself this time ('read the chat and answer them') — read as low effort on an easy, fully-owned problem rather than an LC-200-style ownership gap, since he drove both refactors himself.

pattern.primary: Two Pointers — two independent sorted lists

qualitative.feel: felt easy per him (video-assisted), code correct on first paste, real test was at wrap-up
mistakes: mid-trace boolean-evaluation slip - stated e1<e2 as true when it was actually false, self-corrected in one question
qualitative.key_insight: Advance the pointer whose interval ends first — it's exhausted, can't overlap anything remaining in the other sorted list; the interval with the larger end stays in play for the next comparison.
qualitative.coach_notes: video-assisted, disclosed honestly (same as LC 200). Initially declined the own-words explanation ('I forgot, could you answer that for me') — same disengagement signature as LC 200. Different outcome: given the answer directly, then immediately tested via a fresh concrete trace rather than a verbal recap, and he correctly rebuilt the full reasoning himself within the same session, including self-catching the boolean slip. Meaningfully de-risks vs LC 200 but still logged with a shortened revisit fuse (2026-07-25) requiring own-words explanation before coding at the redo.

mistakes: result.push[next] - bracket notation instead of a call, silently does nothing
push placed unconditionally instead of inside an else branch, corrupting merged result with duplicate entries
missing -1 in the gap formula for adjacent-but-non-overlapping meetings, fixed independently
qualitative.key_insight: Merge overlapping meetings first (classify-and-merge, same as LC 56), then sum gaps: before first, between consecutive merged pairs (with -1 for the off-by-one), and after last.
qualitative.coach_notes: video-assisted, disclosed only at the end. Overrode an offered cold revisit (LC 3) to work on this instead - now 12 straight deferrals. Real debugging happened mid-session: syntax bug given directly on request, structural push-placement bug found via guided trace, and the -1 gap fix was added independently and unprompted - a genuine correctness catch. At wrap-up, declined the ownership-check questions entirely and ended the session ('don't ask me these questions today, going to bed') - unlike LC 986 where the same disengagement was recovered in-session via trace, here there was no recovery attempt. Logged with a short revisit fuse requiring own-words explanation before coding, same protocol as the unresolved LC 200 case.

problem.name: Find the Index of the First Occurrence in a String

qualitative.key_insight: Nested loop, break on first mismatch; outer bound going negative when needle is longer than haystack safely skips the loop entirely.
qualitative.coach_notes: Fully self-written, bug-free brute force solve. KMP introduced conceptually (LPS array, O(m+n)) after a hint did not land and he asked directly - deferred implementing it. Revisit queue offered per protocol at session start and overridden a second straight session (Let's finish this first), now 13 sessions deferred.

qualitative.key_insight: Sum each row, Math.max tracks running best across rows.
qualitative.coach_notes: Trivial, clean, no-bug solve, nothing to teach. Session opened with the LC 3 cold revisit stated as a hard precondition (per prior session recommendation) and overridden twice in a row - third straight session deferring the revisit queue, second distinct framing to fail. Flagged directly that this needs a real conversation with him next session, not another process change.

qualitative.key_insight: Two pointers halve the search space each step; low<=high keeps the single-element window checked.
qualitative.coach_notes: Video-assisted (Alvin's course), disclosed honestly. Code clean, nothing to debug. Declined felt/own-words reflection at wrap-up.

pattern.primary: Binary Search — round up on miss

qualitative.key_insight: Return low, not -1, on a miss — low only advances past elements smaller than target, landing exactly at the insert point.
qualitative.coach_notes: Video-assisted, declined own-words explanation of why low is correct. Given directly, not verified by him.

pattern.primary: Binary Search on Answer — round down on miss

qualitative.key_insight: No real array — search over [0,x], compare mid*mid to x; return high (not low) to round down to the floor of the square root.
qualitative.coach_notes: Video-assisted, declined own-words explanation and declined the LC-986-style trace-recovery offer ('No let's just wrap up'). New data point: trace-recovery isn't universally effective, depends on in-session engagement. Shortened revisit fuse set (2026-07-28) requiring explanation before coding at redo.

problem.name: Find First and Last Position of Element in Sorted Array

qualitative.feel: pretty easy, knew it from the video
qualitative.key_insight: Compose the leftmost-index and rightmost-index helper searches; only one existence check (rightIndex === -1) needed since both search the same array/target.
qualitative.coach_notes: Self-composed from already-built fundamentals helpers, not a fresh video-assisted solve. Declined own-words explanation of the single-existence-check reasoning, asked for it to be written into notes instead - softer variant of the recurring declined-explanation pattern, not a full shutdown. Revisit-queue conversation did not come up at all this session, third straight session with zero traction.

qualitative.feel: pretty easy, same stack pattern from reverseSomeChars, no bugs
qualitative.key_insight: Push target characters (vowels) onto a stack on a first pass, pop them back in reverse order on a second pass. Self-recognized as identical to the stack fundamentals reverseSomeChars pattern, unprompted.
qualitative.coach_notes: First clean instance of a course-fundamentals-module pattern (not curriculum) transferring directly to a real LeetCode problem, in the same session the fundamentals module was built. Clean, correct, no hints needed. Revisit queue still not raised this sub-session.

qualitative.key_insight: Push the expected closer (not the opener) onto the stack; on a closer, check it matches the top of the stack before popping. Self-recognized as identical to the befittingBrackets fundamentals pattern, unprompted, and gave a fully correct own-words explanation unprompted.
qualitative.coach_notes: Second confirmed instance in the same session of a course-fundamentals-module pattern transferring directly to a real LeetCode problem (after LC 345). Now logged in patterns.md as a confirmed 'What's Solid' instinct rather than a single data point. Revisit queue still not raised.

qualitative.feel: pretty straightforward, same stack pattern as decompressBraces
qualitative.key_insight: Pop-until-marker to gather a segment, pop a digit run for a possibly multi-digit count, repeat, push back as one unit so nesting resolves innermost-first. Self-recognized as identical to the decompressBraces fundamentals pattern, unprompted, generalized to multi-digit counts.
qualitative.coach_notes: Third confirmed instance in one session of a course-fundamentals-module pattern transferring directly to a real LeetCode problem, and the first at Medium difficulty requiring real generalization. Declined the live own-words explanation ('you can explain it') - second occurrence of this deflection (after LC 34), not accompanied by broader disengagement. Left one unused helper function as dead code. Discussed but deferred two alternative approaches (recursive descent, two-parallel-stacks). Revisit queue still not raised.

qualitative.feel: easy, exact same pattern as nestingScore
qualitative.key_insight: Seed stack with 0, push 0 on open, pop and fold into new top on close (+1 if popped is 0, else popped*2). Exact unmodified transfer of the nestingScore fundamentals pattern, unlike LC 394 which needed generalization.
qualitative.coach_notes: Fourth and final confirmed instance in one session of a course-fundamentals-module pattern transferring directly to a real LeetCode problem. This closes the transfer-instinct question as fully settled (4/4, both Easy and Medium, both exact-match and generalized). Declined live explanation again, consistent with his own stated mood-dependent preference from earlier this session - not pushed further. Revisit queue still not raised.

qualitative.feel: watched a NeetCode video first, pretty easy once the idea of the second stack clicked
mistakes: pop() referenced an undefined value variable, leftover from push's parameter
qualitative.key_insight: Run a parallel minStack tracking the running minimum at each depth alongside the main stack; getMin() becomes an O(1) peek. Self-derived the space-optimized variant's pop mechanism (pop minStack only when popped value equals its top) through pure Socratic questioning, implementation deferred.
qualitative.coach_notes: Video-assisted (disclosed honestly), but unlike LC 200/LC 3169 showed real engagement: self-caught a genuine bug in one guided question, and fully reasoned through a harder follow-up conceptual extension (space optimization) with zero direct answers given. Positive counter-example to the video-assisted-predicts-disengagement worry. Revisit queue still not raised.

qualitative.feel: pretty easy, near one-shot except the truncation bug
mistakes: used Math.floor instead of Math.trunc for integer division, fails on negative results
qualitative.key_insight: Push operands, on an operator pop right-then-left operand, apply, push result back. Math.trunc truncates toward zero regardless of sign, unlike Math.floor (rounds down) or Math.round (rounds to nearest).
qualitative.coach_notes: Near one-shot solve, correctly self-identified the core RPN mechanism unhinted. One real bug (Math.floor vs Math.trunc), guided through a trace, one wrong guess (Math.round) before asking directly. New toolkit-recall gap logged (patterns.md #70). Honestly self-attributed the speed to prior exposure, not overconfidence. Revisit queue still not raised, sixth problem today with zero mention.

qualitative.feel: smooth, clicked fast once he thought of sets
mistakes: result = [] declared with no const/let, implicit global - recurrence of patterns.md #10
qualitative.key_insight: Sets remove duplicates within each array for free and give O(1) membership checks; the dedup step is specifically a response to the duplicate-elements constraint - correctly reasoned that without that constraint, no Set would be needed at all.
qualitative.coach_notes: Self-solved, no hints on the algorithm. Third recurrence of the implicit-global bug across 6 weeks (LC 167/977 -> LC 349), different variable name each time - still not self-caught. Confirmed the O(n+m) solution is already optimal when he asked about a better way; mentioned ES2024 Set.prototype.intersection as a modern alternative. Revisit queue explicitly not raised per his direct request this session.

problem.name: Find the Difference of Two Arrays

qualitative.key_insight: Mirror image of LC 349 (Intersection) - Sets to kill duplicates, walk each Set keeping non-members of the other, in both directions.
qualitative.coach_notes: Second problem of the same hashing sweep as LC 349, same day. Clean, self-written, no bugs, unprompted own-words explanation. No implicit-global slip this time, unlike LC 349 immediately before - single clean data point, not yet closed as resolved. Revisit queue not raised, per explicit instruction earlier the same session.

pattern.primary: Array Rotation - Index Mapping via Modulo

qualitative.feel: confident on brute force and TLE diagnosis, harder climb on the optimization; modulo-wraparound didn't feel familiar
mistakes: O(n*k) brute force via repeated unshift
first extra-array draft returned instead of mutating nums in place
qualitative.key_insight: Destination index (i+k)%n via modulo wraparound; independently spotted that writing to that index in place would overwrite unread values (aliasing) before being asked - genuine unprompted correctness catch.
qualitative.coach_notes: Mixed session: strong independent TLE diagnosis and an unprompted aliasing catch, but modulo-for-wraparound concept and both O(1)-space follow-ups (three reversals, cyclic replacement) needed full direct teaching at his explicit request ('teach me like a baby'). Declined wrap-up reflection questions - not treated as a red flag given the strong independent work, but shorter revisit fuse set specifically to cold-test the two taught approaches. Revisit queue not raised, per standing instruction.
